messages.content: Where were you when the blackout hit?

In the archive corridor, trying to preserve some order while everyone else lost theirs. I remember a heavy thud somewhere down the east wing, but in that darkness it could have been any careless fool dropping equipment.
Did you touch the stone tonight?
The stone is a 9th-century granite slab, not a paperweight one casually rearranges between meetings. I examined it earlier under proper supervision, and that is the full extent of my contact.
What did you hear during the blackout?
Mostly panic, shuffling, and one very distinct heavy thud that did not belong in a professional lab. I noticed it because unlike the others, I was still capable of listening.
People say you seemed too calm.
Composure is often mistaken for guilt by people who are more familiar with drama than discipline. If I appeared calm, it is because somebody in that building had to remain intelligent.
Why mention the stone's weight so often?
Because facts matter, investigator. A 9th-century granite artifact has considerable mass, which makes the childish theories about it being whisked away in seconds rather difficult to respect.
Did you see Leo during the outage?
Briefly, yes. He looked exactly as he usually does under pressure: uncertain, overburdened, and one question away from apologizing to the walls.
Could one person have moved the stone alone?

A determined person can do many things, though moving a 9th-century granite slab without preparation would be awkward and loud. Which is why that heavy thud during the blackout remains more interesting than the speculation around it.
Why didn't you report the sound immediately?

Because the building was in chaos and I was more concerned with restoring order than narrating every noise for the less observant. Had I known people would later confuse restraint with omission, perhaps I would have provided a running commentary.
Are you hiding something about the blackout?
Only my impatience. I have told you repeatedly that I heard a heavy thud and little else of substance, and I refuse to embroider the truth simply because you find plain answers unsatisfying.
Describe the stone for me.
An important 9th-century granite inscription, historically significant and physically cumbersome. Anyone who handled it carelessly would remember the weight long before they remembered the wording.
Did the blackout help the thief?
Obviously. Darkness makes amateurs frantic and gives opportunists cover, though it also amplifies mistakes. A heavy object striking the floor, for instance, is hardly the mark of perfect planning.
You sound annoyed by these questions.
Annoyance is an understandable response to imprecision. If you intend to accuse me, do try to do it with something sturdier than tone and coincidence.
What do you think happened to the stone?
I think someone exploited the blackout, underestimated the 9th-century granite weight of the artifact, and made more noise than they intended. Beyond that, I prefer evidence to melodrama.
Did you carry any heavy equipment tonight?
No. I had my notes, my keys, and enough responsibility to occupy me without moonlighting as a porter. If something heavy moved, it was not by my hand.
Give me one detail you keep coming back to.
The heavy thud during the blackout. Not because it is theatrical, but because it suggests weight, mishandling, and someone far less composed than I am.
